headline: Lynas Moves 450,000 Tonnes of Waste to Gebeng
context: Lynas Malaysia disclosed in March 2025 that 450,000 tonnes of water leach purification residue remain stored at its Gebeng facility pending permanent disposal approval.

cards.big: Lynas says its residue is 'naturally occurring and safe.'
cards.sub: The IAEA classifies the same material as radioactive residue requiring regulated long-term storage.

cards.big: AELB extended Lynas's licence 6 times since 2012.
cards.sub: Each extension deferred the permanent disposal facility deadline. No penalty was ever imposed.

cards.big: Gebeng residents report 23% higher thorium levels in groundwater.
cards.sub: DOE's own 2024 monitoring data shows readings above WHO guidelines at 3 of 7 stations.

cards.big: Lynas contributes RM300M annually to Pahang's GDP.
cards.sub: The economic argument has consistently overridden environmental caution in cabinet discussions since 2012.

cards.big: If the waste were safe, why has no permanent site been approved in 13 years?
The issue is not whether rare earths matter — they do. The issue is whether indefinite temporary storage is an acceptable regulatory outcome.
